coredata.dc:description: The bacterial exopolysaccharides (EPS) enticed the researchers because of its natural protection against predators, and adhesion to the surface. Thus, this study was conducted for the EPS production from Exiguobacterium sp. VK2, the synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dot (CQD) from EPS and it was further used for bioenergy production. The CQD was synthesized and characterized using hydrothermal reaction and its structure was elucidated by several methods like Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, UV–visible spectroscopy, Gas chromatography-Mass spectroscopy (GC-MS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), HR-TEM, SAED (Selected Area Electron Diffraction) spectrum, Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Brunaur-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis. Further, the synthesized CQD from EPS of Exiguobacterium sp.VK2 was tested for its catalytic activity in biodiesel production from waste cooking oil (WCO) along with two different conventional catalysts and more than 90% of total fatty acid methyl ester (FAME) was achieved by CQD than the control.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots (CQD) fabricated from Exiguobacterium sp. VK2 exopolysaccharide (EPS) using hydrothermal reaction and its biodiesel applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The bacterial exopolysaccharides (EPS) enticed the researchers because of its natural protection against predators, and adhesion to the surface. Thus, this study was conducted for the EPS production from Exiguobacterium sp. VK2, the synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dot (CQD) from EPS and it was further used for bioenergy production. The CQD was synthesized and characterized using hydrothermal reaction and its structure was elucidated by several methods like Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, UV–visible spectroscopy, Gas chromatography-Mass spectroscopy (GC-MS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), HR-TEM, SAED (Selected Area Electron Diffraction) spectrum, Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Brunaur-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis. Further, the synthesized CQD from EPS of Exiguobacterium sp.VK2 was tested for its catalytic activity in biodiesel production from waste cooking oil (WCO) along with two different conventional catalysts and more than 90% of total fatty acid methyl ester (FAME) was achieved by CQD than the control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Exopolysaccharides (EPS) are extracted from the bacteria, fungi, algae and plant that possess different sugar constituents at various proportions [1]. The functionalized EPS defends the bacteria against biotic and abiotic temperature, pH, light intensity stresses. The foremost role of EPS is to protect the organism and it also allows the organism to tolerate the higher temperature. Herewith, the EPS attained from various sources has been used in different industries such as pharmaceutical, cosmetics industries, and food products. So far, the EPS reported from Exiguobacterium sp., showed tolerance towards high temperature habitat and it also involved in degradation of naphthalene, aliphatic hydrocarbon.

Recently, the zero-dimensional (0D) CQD fascinated the researchers due of its several applications such as photocatalysis, solar cells, fuel cells, optoelectronic devices, drug delivery, bio-labeling, and bio-imaging applications [2,3]. The carbon-based fluorescent materials have specialized features like high electrical conductivity, chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, higher quantum size effect, and high photo luminescent properties [4,5].

To date, the hike of petroleum consumption and fossil fuel demand urge to an alternate fuel synthesis by means of using different feedstocks like lignocellulosic biomass [6], waste oils [7], algal biomass microalgae [8] and macroalgae [9] etc. Owing to this, the bio fuel production is carried out by using several modes like conventional, thermochemical methods like hydrothermal liquefaction and pyrolysis [10]. Among this waste oil was obtained from worldwide more than 1 gallon that deliberately pollutes the environment. Thus, the waste cooking oil (WCO) has been considered as both remediation and feedstock purpose. The choice of WCO is due to its availability and also in concern of avoiding the water pollution caused by direct release of WCO into water bodies [7]. Further, the enhancing of biodiesel yield was achieved by using different catalysts such as homogeneous (NaOH, KOH and CH3ONa) and heterogeneous (alkali metal, alkali metal oxides, mixed metal oxides and Ion exchange resins) is used to enhance the transesterification process [11]. Generally, CQD is highly stable in thermal conditions, and its metal free composition ensures its potential and the route of synthesis is economically feasible on comparing with other catalyst [12]. Moreover, the stability and multifaceted functional activities of carbon dot involved in regulating the physio-chemical properties that facilitates catalytic activity during transesterification [13]. Recently, there was a report dealt with CQD as catalyst synthesized from glycine and citric acid using hydrothermal process for canola oil transesterification. The CQD’s physiochemical feature becomes efficient in catalytic functions [14]. Therefore, this study dealt with the application of EPS derived CQD in biodiesel production as a catalyst using waste cooking oil (WCO) as a major feedstock. So, this research majorly focused on characterization of CQD from the EPS of Exiguobacterium sp. VK2, and exploration of its potential as catalyst in biodiesel production from WCO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Screening of extremophilic bacteria from salt pan for EPS production
ce:section.ce:para: The samples were collected from salt pan soil from Tuticorin, Tamil Nadu, India (8°45.43′N and 78°9.715′ E). Then, 1 g of soil samples were subjected to serial dilution (10−1 to 10−8) and plated on the Zobell marine agar medium (Hi Media) along with 0.8 g/L congo red and incubated for 48 h at 37˚C in the incubator. The isolate was selected based on specific coloration of colonies as an indication of the EPS production [15] and used for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular characterization of halophilic bacteria using 16S rRNA analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Total genomic DNA was retrieved from isolate VK2 using the modified method [16]. The amplification of the 16S rRNA region of the isolated DNA was done by using universal primer 27 FP-5′-AGAGTTTGATCCTGGCTCAG-3′ and 1492RP-5′-GGTTACCTTGTTACGACTT-3′ through polymerase chain reaction (PCR) (Nexus gradient master cycler, Eppendorf, Germany) with the subsequent reaction conditions such as initial denaturation at 94℃ for 5 min, continued by 35 cycles of denaturation at 94℃ for 1 min, annealing at 60℃ for 1 min, extension for 1 min at 72℃, and a final extension at 72℃ for 10 min. Then the PCR products were analyzed by Sanger’s dideoxy method of sequencing and BLAST analysis was carried out with the provided 16S rRNA region as the query sequence. A phylogenetic tree was constructed based on the neighbor-joining tree method (MEGA 6 software) and matched with the closely related ribosomal sequences of bacteria to identify the genera of the isolate VK2.

The well isolated black-colored colony was selected and further cultivated in Zobell marine broth (HiMedia) with proper agitation at 200 rpm for 48 h to reach the stationary phase. Then, the cells were harvested by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 20 min at 4℃. To the supernatant threefold ice-cold acetone was added and kept overnight at 4℃ to attain complete precipitation [17]. The precipitated EPS was then recovered by centrifugation at 6000 rpm for 10 min at 4℃. The precipitated EPS was dialyzed with double distilled water using 12 kDa size membranes at 4℃ for 48 h. The extracted EPS was further subjected to estimate the total carbohydrates by modified phenol sulphuric acid assay [18] using microtiter plate and wherein 50 µl of EPS solution with unknown concentration was hydrolyzed with 150 µl of concentrated sulphuric acid and 30 µl of 5% aqueous phenol solution was added to these contents and kept for 5 min at 90˚C to get the colored end product. Upon cooling to ambient temperature, the absorbance was taken at 490 nm in ELISA plate reader (Thermo scientific multi skan EX ELISA reader, Finland).

The main functional groups of the partially purified EPS were identified using FT-IR spectroscopy (Bruker Optic, Germany). The pellets were prepared by pressing 1:100 (v/v) of the EPS powder against Potassium Bromide (KBr) into a 16 mm mold. The spectrum was recorded in a region of 4000–400 cm−1 by placing the frame on the sample holder. The range includes wave numbers of various functional groups of the polysaccharide [19].

The TGA was performed in SDT Q600 V8.3 Build 101 unit. The thermogram was obtainedataflowrateof100 mL/minbetween30 and 400℃inatmospheric nitrogen, direct heating at a rate of 10℃/min, and raised to 100–600℃. EPS sample (2.984 mg) was kept in Al2O3 crucible. Meanwhile, theEPSweightlosswas also recorded. The CuSO4·5H2O was utilized to calibrate temperature readings [20].

The composition of EPS produced by Exiguobacterium sp. VK2 was analyzed by GC–MS. The EPS (3 mg) was hydrolyzed at 120℃ for 2 h in 3 mL of 4 M trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) (0.918 mL of TFA and 2.08 mL of water) to obtain monosaccharides, which was derivatized to equivalent alditol acetates by treatment at 100℃ with 0.1 M toluene (0.5 mL) and 0.1 mL acetic anhydride. This was achieved by the use of sodium borohydride (NaBH4), acetylated with acetic anhydride, and pyridine [21]. The derivatized sample (1 µl) was then injected into the variant column of GC–MS (JEOL GC MATE-II; JEOL ltd., Tokyo, Japan), and the m/z values was obtained. The system was fitted with column (HP-5 MS), photon multiplier tube detector, and double-focusing quadrupole mass analyzer. The flow rate of 1 mL/min, helium gas was used as the carrier gas. Oven temperature was held for 2 min at 50℃, increased to 250℃ at a rate of 10 ℃/min. TemperaturesfortheGCinjectorandchamberionswas setat250℃. Peaks obtained were compared with the National Institute for Standards and Technology (NIST) library.

The minimal media was supplemented with 1% of sugarcane leaf, pineapple peel, and cassava peel as carbon supplements. After sterilization, the media was added with 1% (1 mL in 99 mL) fresh inoculum of Exiguobacterium sp. VK2 culture. Then, the inoculated culture was grown for 48 h and collected at stationary phase to analyze the EPS content in the culture supernatant. All these experiments were performed in triplicates.

One-step hydrothermal carbonization method was used to fabricate CQDs from the purified EPS of Exiguobacterium sp. VK2. To perform this, VK2 EPS (1.0 g) was dissolved in 20 mL double-distilled water, transferred to 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and incubated at 200℃ for 24 h. A dark brown solution obtained was cool down to room temperature (37°C) and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane, to remove the large particles or aggregates. Further, the CQD solution was dialyzed in a membrane (1 kDa) against double distilled water for 48 h, and stored at 4℃ for further use. This CQD solution was then dried and stored for further use [1].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQD from exopolysaccharide of Exiguobacterium sp.VK2
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical functionalization of EPS-derived CQD was detected using FT-IR (Spectrum GX-1, Perkin Elmer, and USA), scanned in the spectral region of 4000–400 cm−1. Further, the crystalline nature of the CQD was performed at XRD using Philips Electronic Instruments (Inc., Mahwah, NJ) with a copper target (Cu Kα1, λ = 1.54056 Å). Then, the UV–vis absorbance spectrum was recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu-1600). The SAED of CQD was also analyzed using HR-TEM (TECNAI F30) at a voltage of 80 kV. Further, the surface morphology, intermittent contact mode was detected by AFM (BT02218 Nanosurf, Switzerland). The specific surface area and pore size was recorded by using BET model correspondingly. The chemical elemental analysis of CQD was carried out using XPS that was performed with a Japan Kratos Axis ultra HAS spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodiesel production from waste cooking oil
ce:section.ce:para: The CQD was evaluated for its potential using WCO. For transesterification, 40 µl of waste cooking oil, the CQD synthesized from bacteria was added in different concentrations viz, 1% (0.3 mg), 3% (0.9 mg) and 5% (1.5 mg) and 0.9 mL, 2.7 mL and 4.5 mL of methanol were also added along with the CQD 1.5 mL of toluene was added, 150 µl of acid (HCl) and base (NaOH) was added to all the different concentration of CQD. Further, the following set up was kept in a water bath for 2 h to allow transesterification. After that, the sample was added with hexane and kept in a shaker for overnight incubation. Then, the upper phase of hexane was collected in GC vials and 0.5 µl of standard Triacyl glycerol (TAG) was added. Finally, the sample was evaluated for fatty acid methyl esters (FAME) using GC-MS analysis [22,23].

The salt pan isolate VK2 was characterized by amplification of 16S rRNA region using universal primers. The 16S rRNA sequence was analyzed (SciGenom Labs Private ltd., Cochin) (1421 bp) using BLAST and showed 100 % sequence similarity to Exiguobacterium sp. The sequence was evaluated by phylogenetic tree to constitute its link with its closely related species using MEGA software (Version 6) and showed that the closely related species was Exiguobacterium profundum (Fig. 1 a) (Genbank Accession number- MH229359).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological identification of Exiguobacterium sp. VK2
ce:section.ce:para: The isolate VK2 was examined for its structural difference based on Gram staining which enunciating that it was small Gram-positive Bacillus and it was similar to the previous report as the cell wall of Exiguobacterium genus isolated from salt pan showed differentiation in its structure like rod shaped during young cultures and form coccoid structure in old cultures. Earlier, the alkalophilic bacterium had screened from potato-processing effluent and it was non-spore forming, gram-positive, motile bacteria which diverged in shape from short rod (exponential phase) to coccoid forms during the stationary phase [24]. Later, these alkalophilic bacteria were placed under new genus Exiguobacterium based on chemotaxonomic study [25].

The EPS extracted from Exiguobacterium sp.VK2 used in nano science applications, like metal nano particles in an eco-friendly way [26] stabilizers, further it also acts as bioactive carriers for DNA, proteins and drugs [27,62].

The catalytic and free radical quenching ability of EPS was specifically depending on its functional groups available in its structure. The spectrum indicated that the presence of a carbonyl group at 1653 cm−1 wave number. At a wave number of 3446 cm−1, a dominant adsorption relating to hydroxyl groups was identified. The wave numbers 2922 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1 were matched to methyl and methylene groups correspondingly. A narrow, sharp vibration stretch at 1741 cm−1 belonged to the polysaccharide COC group (Fig. 1b). The obtained spectrum in our results were in accordance with the previous reports [28]. The results obtained through FT-IR were substantiated with EPS of the Exiguobacterium sp. VK2 consisted of galactose, glucose, mannose, and xylose, which was further verified by GC–MS analysis. Conclusively, it can be predicted that obtained EPS is a polysaccharide whose thermal stability assessed by TGA.

TGA resolves the loss of substance by applying temperature dynamically. The EPS thermal scrutiny was achieved by heat emission and absorption, together with a transition in the polymer structure and crystalline polymer melting [29]. An initial mass loss of 17.65 % was achieved at 241.67℃ due to the moisture and alcohol content in the EPS, which contributed to the broad hydroxyl extend in FTIR analysis. A significant mass loss of around 38 % was reported at approximately 336.86 ℃ [30]. The mass loss persists until a 34.01 % weight of EPS was measured at 607.14℃, and 26.37 % residue (0.7869 mg) remained even after 1000℃ (Fig. 1c). This data inferred that the EPS is thermally very stable [31]. Thermostable characteristics of EPS play a significant role in the food industry especially in improving rheological properties in the manufacturing and processing of numerous food preparations because that are carried out at higher temperature [32]. Thermal stability, in combination with other physical properties such as viscosity, may aid the EPS of Rhodobacterjohrii RH-7 screened from the surface of the marine macroalga Padina sp. to discover appropriate uses in varieties of industries, including the food and cosmetics industries [31]. A unique exopolysaccharide was produced by halophilic bacterial strain obtained from an artificial Salt Lake in Romania with a melting temperature of 207°C, that polymer had an excellent thermostability [33]. Generally, in TGA the first phase occurred between 50°C and 150°C that was correlated with the loss of moisture while, the dried EPS was taken and it was not strictly anhydrous. In the second phase depolymerization begins from 200°C, continued to 400°C and steady phase was observed with residual EPS [34].

For instance, the EPS produced by Leuconostoc lactis (KC117496) screened from idly batter, an Indian acidic fermented food was subjected to TGA. The initial weight loss of up to 15% occurred from 0 to 100 ℃ because of the existence of moisture. Then the second stage of degradation started (22.82%) from 100 to 240℃. The huge loss occurred at 240–400 ℃ (58.56%). It is due to the carboxyl group in EPS is coupled to more water molecules, it might be accelerated the breakdown in this phase. When the temperature reached 241.14℃ causes total weight loss of the EPS. The thermal change of EPS from its amorphous to crystalline state was significant in this range of the exothermic process. In another report, the EPS was degraded at 272.01℃ [35] which was much less when compared to the present study, where only 34.01% weight loss of EPS was measured at 607.14℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas chromatography – Mass spectrometry (GC–MS) of EPS from Exiguobacterium sp. VK2
ce:section.ce:para: In earlier report, the monosaccharide content of EPS from Lactobacillus plantarum YW32 was determined by GC–MS and revealed that the EPS was made up of several sugar monomers such as mannose, fructose, galactose, and glucose, indicating that the EPS was a heteropolysaccharide [36]. Whereas, the monomers from the repetitive unit of EPS synthesized by Bacillus velezensis was examined by GC–MS, showed one major peak at 13.59 min and smaller peaks at 9.73 and 5.36 min. The MS fragmentation patterns formed by each peak from GC were analyzed and revealed the characteristics of aldohexoses such as glucose, galactose, and mannose in sugar alditol acetate form. The MS was developed and correlated to the spectrum of different monomers to identify each peak [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different carbon sources in the EPS production
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the ingredients in media play a pivotal role in bacterial fermentation. Therefore, using of sustainable and inexpensive raw material gives a quality end-product. In concern with these, the readily available Luria broth and Zobell marine broth were replaced with theminimal media containing naturally available nutrients in agro-industrial wastes (cassava peel, sugarcane leaf and pine apple peel) at 1% concentration as carbon source. Likewise, Bacillus strain CMG1403 was grown on neutral minimal medium supplemented with locally available cheap sources such as 20 g of glucose, sweet whey, cane molasses, sucrose fructose and sugar beet yielded EPS of about 3.82 ± 0.005, 2.71 ± 0.024, 4.73 ± 0.021, 4.85 ± 0.024, 4.33 ± 0.021, and 5.52 ± 0.016 g/L. Therefore, the results implies under an ideal growing conditions, low-cost substrates shown an excellent and economically alternate to synthetic carbon sources that would be allowed for manufacturing of water-absorbing EPS at a lower cost [40]. Similarly, it was reported that the Halobacillus trueperi AJSK cultivated using the Zobell marine agar as a suitable media for halophilic bacteria but later, a broth containing peptone, glucose, NaCl and MgSO4 was adopted as production media for EPS [41].

As a staple food, cassava (Manihot esculenta Crantz) was also a drought-tolerant crop [42]. The processing of cassava produces massive amounts of waste that could cause environmental harm if it is not properly disposed [43]. The cassava peels were toxic to flora and fauna because of its cyanogenic potential [44]. The improper peel retention for lengthy periods of time, especially during the rainy season was reported as the major issue. The low digestion and toxicity from an extraordinarily high quantity of hydrocyanic acid limit its use. Fermentation decreases the toxicity of cassava peel and converting into the enzyme-resistant lignocellulose substance into a more digestible form [45]. The unpleasant odour, perhaps deadly and contaminated air produced by the fermentation of cassava peels from such heaps [46]. Furthermore, the rapid post-harvest decay, deterioration, low protein content, and environmental impacts could be caused by the effluent and other related wastes that offer an aesthetic nuisance, so it needs a proper management wastes [47]. These drawbacks alarmed us to disposal or reuse of solid waste from the cassava processing industry needs to be proper. Llamas noted that microbial source of EPSs occurred in many ecosystems and played a crucial role in the formation of biofilms, and that enable the attachment of cells to the surfaces and also shielding bacteria from hazardous environmental influences [48]. Hence in this study, the nutritionally rich, cost-effective but relatively toxic, sun-dried cassava peel was powdered, and utilized as substrate for the EPS production of Exiguobacterium sp. VK2. The outcome showed that the yield of EPS (7.96 g/L) was higher in cassavas peel (1%) supplementation rather than other agro-wastes (Fig. 3 ).

Sugar cane (Saccharum officinarum) leaf consisted of lignin, cellulose, hemicelluloses [49,50]. Exiguobacterium sp. could synthesize lignocellulolytic enzymes to degrade the complex polysaccharides [51]. Therefore, a sugarcane leaf was supplemented as additional nutrient source in the minimal medium to enhance the EPS production in this study. But the bacterial growth and EPS production was drastically affected while compared to sucrose and cassava peel supplementation. The presence of phenolic compounds in the sugarcane leaf was inhibit the bacterial growth.Hence, the sugarcane leave powder was not supplemented further to produce EPS in further studies.

Owing the these, pineapple (Ananas comosus) peel waste contained substantial amounts of soluble sugars, high fiber content, and low protein content. Pineapple peel composed of tannins, cardiac glycosides, terpenoids, along with reducing sugars [52]. Though it contains high nutritional values, it cannot be utilized for feed purpose due to its toxic nature to the environment [53]. Further, the Ananas waste was recommended as a vast source of organic raw materials and could potentially turned into useful products by waste bioprocessing [54]. In our report, the result evidently indicated that the bacterial growth retardation was observed and EPS production (1502.745 mg/L) becomes low. This might be due to the antimicrobial activity of pineapple peel that was also confirmed by previous reports, A. comosus peel extract has a prohibitive effect on microbes such as B. cereus, S. aureus, E. coli, K. pneumonia [55] , Streptococcus lactis, Pseudomonas aeruginosa [52] . In another study, the Li et al. [56] was identified and quantified various polyphenolic compounds in pineapple peels. The combination of ferulic acid-epicatechin and ferulic acid-gallic acid were also showing antioxidant and antibacterial activities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of EPS-derived carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorbance intensity of as synthesized EPS derived CQDs was characterized by UV–Visible spectrophotometer. The UV–vis spectra of EPS CQDs were showed the sharp absorbance peak at 342 nm which was corresponded to the n-π* transition of carbonyl groups (CO) [57] as shown in Fig. 4 (a). Besides, a high degree of graphitization, crystal behavior, and structure of carbon in prepared CQDs was confirmed using Raman studies. As given in Fig. 4 (b) the two various peaks appeared were due to D-band at 1334 cm−1 and corresponded to disorder graphitic carbon, further G-band exhibited at 1578 cm−1 due to sp2 hybridization of graphite core [53,54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical functionalization and crystalline phase analysis
ce:section.ce:para: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent-based CQD was synthesized and characterized through FTIR spectroscopy Fig. 4 (c). It was revealed that the absorbance bands at 3651 cm−1 and 2922 cm−1 represented to stretching vibration of OH and CH peaks of the CQD, respectively. The height at 1743 cm−1 attributed to the CO carbonyl stretch. Similarly, the sharp absorbance peak at 1651 cm−1, 1541 cm−1, and 1029 cm−1 was assigned to CC, CN, and CO stretching vibrations of CQDs [60]. The XRD study was performed to recognize the crystalline nature of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 (d), a firm diffraction peaks were observed at the 2θ value of = 31 Å (002-plane) which was indicating that highly disorder graphitic atoms of CQDs, the corresponding d-spacing pace calculated to be 0.28 nm, it had been equated to previously reported CQDs [61].

The surface morphology of the CQDs was examined by HR-TEM and AFM analysis. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), a distinctive HR-TEM image of as-prepared CQD, demonstrated a relatively quasi-spherical alike shape at an average size range of particular CQD, was nearly 20 nm using image J software Fig. 5 (b) represented SAED spectrum of CQD, witnessed white spots of the CQD were relatively crystalline nature, and the same pattern was observed in previous reports [62]. Further, the AFM images of CQDs was correlated with HR-TEM analysis; Fig. 5 (c-e) demonstrated that the topographical height profile of CQDs was about 9.96 nm with a thickness of 16.3 nm representing that the CQDs obtained were almost same spherical like structure [63].

The chemical nature of the EPS-derived CQDs and interaction of elements was investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Fig. 6 ). From the XPS results, Fig. 6 (a) the spectrum indicated that EPS-derived CQDs having 55.79 C%, 28.64 O%, 7.59 N%, 3.88 S%, 2.89 Na%, and 1.2 Mg%. As given in Fig. 6 (b), the predominant graphitic C1s peak at 283, 284, and 286 eV corresponded to groups of C-(CH), C-(NO), and (CO) respectively [58,59]. The O1s peaks Fig. 6 (c) at 530 and 532 eV could be assigned the groups of (CO) and (CO) [65]. The N1s peaks Fig. 6 (d) at 398 eV ascribed to (CNC) and (NH) represented the amine nitrogen and amido nitrogen of EPS-derived CQDs [66]. Although the S2p peak at 168 eV (-CS-) indicated the presence of sulfur groups, moreover, the two peaks placed at 495 and 305 eV might be presented the Na and Mg groups respectively [67].

The surface area and porosity of the CQDs was determined using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis. The nitrogen adsorption and desorption curve of EPS-derived CQDs was presented in Fig.7 . The curve indicated type IV isotherm corresponds to the existence of the mesoporous nature of the nanoparticles. In respect to highlight the potential value of CQD as catalytic materials, the BET specific surface area, pore-size distribution, and pore volume for pure MH and CQD/MH hybrid clusters were calculated using their respective nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms [68]. Further, the EPS-derived CQDs in BET surface area of CQDs were 4.64 m2/g, and the pore size distribution was calculated by using a model from adsorption branches of the isotherms. The pore size and pore volume of CQDs was 2.76 nm and 0.006 cc/g respectively, as shown in the inset image of Fig. 7
[63,64]. The nitrogen adsorption and desorption curve of EPS-derived CQDs was presented in Fig. 7. The obtained curve indicated that type IV isotherm corresponds to existence of the mesoporous nature of the EPS-derived CQDs was well correlated with previous report of Q. Chang et al., 2019 carbon dots also have type IV isotherm, which confirming that the prepared EPS-derived CQDs is a mesoporous in nature [69,70].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FAME analysis of CQD in waste cooking oil
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the CO2 emission in the world was an emergent threat that enticing the researchers to minimize the CO2 via developing many novel strategies in bio energy applications. In this concern, the CQD was employed as catalyst due to its distinctive features in structure like non-toxic, freely bounded on the surface and enlarged its surface area that provides, storage and bids an active site to improve the electrocatalytic performance [1]. Further, CQD also has stability, and it contains an alcohol and carboxylate parts in its surface that renders for transesterification. The amount of WCO thrust out to the environment that pollutes the water. For example, 1 L of oil can easily affect the 5 lakhs L of natural water. In concern with this peril, the conversion of WCO to biodiesel was already carried out and yielded 1.8 million L by some private companies in US and Japan. Thus, the current study focused on utilizing the WCO to improve the biodiesel yield through CQD applications. WCO as a heterogeneous catalyst and the two other different homogeneous catalysts (HCl and NaOH) had shown a different percentage of biogas yields. The experimental results (Fig. 8) were clearly evidenced that the one fold increase of PUFA (34%) by CQD with base (NaOH) mediated transesterification in waste cooking oil when compared to the control (16%). But in case of acid transesterification with CQD shown meager increase of PUFA (21%) content than the control (12%). Besides, this the saturated fatty acid (SFA) and mono unsaturated fatty acid (MUFA) was not prominent than the control. The total FAME content was increased in control of both acid and base mediated transesterification compared to the CQD treated. Meanwhile, the CQD along with an acid (HCl) produces 87.27 % but use of base (NaOH) catalyst produces 96.97 % of total FAME. In one of the earlier reports, the carbon dot synthesized from glycine used as catalyst (1%) in transesterifying the canola oil showed 98.3% of biodiesel production [14].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biodiesel quality parameters of CQD function in WCO
ce:section.ce:para: Biodiesel quality parameters were defined based on the standards of American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) and European Standards (EN14214). In concerning with these standards, the oxidative stability was determined by the presence of unsaturated fatty acids and Iodine value (IV). From the experimental results, the fuel characteristics of biodiesel obtained from CQD along with base catalyst meet out the international standards. While discussing about the saponification value (SV), in both reaction of CQD with an acid and base exhibited within the limits of 120 than the control. The cetane number (CN) was calculated to determine the ignition quality of the fuel. From the experimental results, it clearly evidenced that the ignition quality is higher and faster. The Degree of unsaturation was also convinced with the standards while CQD reacted with base. The flow acquisition of biodiesel was calculated through the Cold filter plugging point (CFPP) and long chain saturation factor (LCSF) were witnessed that the obtained result from the CQD with acid and base catalyst as a suitable biodiesel (Table 2 ).

Precisely, the exopolysaccharide production from Exiguobacterium sp. VK2 was optimized using different sources. Further, the CQD obtained from exopolysaccharide was used as catalyst in biodiesel production and yielded 96.67% of total FAME. Thus, the results clearly depicted that the efficiency of CQD as a catalyst for biodiesel production. This is the first report using Exiguobacterium sp. VK2 for EPS and biodiesel production as per our knowledge. Further, this study exhibits a new outlook on carbon quantum dot synthesized from exopolysaccharides of bacteria.
